skill.name: vercel-react-best-practices
skill.description: React and Next.js performance guidance for writing, reviewing, and refactoring applications with Vercel-style best practices.
skill.summary: Apply practical React and Next.js performance rules for faster, cleaner applications.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many script, command, network, sensitive-data, weak-crypto, and obfuscation indicators. Manual review found these indicators are markdown examples, documentation links, or lexical matches in a React and Next.js best-practices guide, not executable skill behavior. No prompt injection attempt or malicious intent was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static script indicators are documentation examples
low_findings.description: Dynamic import and next/dynamic patterns appear inside markdown guidance for bundle optimization. They are examples for application code and are not executed by this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns are inside fenced markdown examples under bundle optimization sections. No executable JavaScript source file or runtime loader was found for the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Command-like tokens are markdown instructions
low_findings.description: Backtick and command detections come from README and SKILL documentation, including example pnpm commands and file names. They do not form an automatic command execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain markdown showing setup or usage instructions. I found no script file or code path that invokes these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Network and browser storage findings are illustrative examples
low_findings.description: Fetch, localStorage, and hardcoded URL hits occur in code examples or reference metadata. The reviewed files use them to teach data fetching, storage minimization, and documentation references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and storage patterns are contained in teaching examples or static reference lists. No evidence shows data exfiltration or runtime collection by the skill.

low_findings.title: Sensitive-data and weak-crypto alerts are lexical false positives
low_findings.description: Sensitive-key alerts match keyboard event variable names, and weak-crypto alerts match performance terms such as min and max. These are not credentials or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context shows keyboard shortcut handling and numeric min/max examples. No private key material, certificate, hash implementation, or secret handling was present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation heuristic is caused by markdown structure
low_findings.description: The JSFuck-style bracket heuristic appears to be triggered by the compiled markdown table of contents and links. The content is a readable guide, not obfuscated executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious structure is normal markdown headings and links, plus a documented JavaScript array method name. I found no encoded payload or code execution wrapper.

content.value_statement: React and Next.js apps often lose speed through waterfalls, large bundles, and avoidable re-renders. This skill gives agents concrete rules for safer performance-focused changes.
actual_capabilities: Applies rules for eliminating async waterfalls in React and Next.js code.
Guides bundle-size improvements with direct imports, dynamic imports, and deferred libraries.
Recommends server-side patterns for caching, serialization, Suspense, and Server Actions.
Identifies common client-side data fetching and event listener inefficiencies.
Suggests re-render, rendering, and JavaScript performance improvements with before and after examples.
limitations: It is guidance content, not an automated profiler or benchmark tool.
It does not replace framework documentation for version-specific behavior.
Some recommendations require project context before they should be applied.
It does not install dependencies or modify build configuration by itself.

use_cases.title: Review a React Pull Request
use_cases.description: Find waterfall, re-render, and bundle risks before a frontend change ships.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Next.js Page
use_cases.description: Use Suspense, parallel fetching, and slimmer client props to improve page performance.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Output
use_cases.description: Give Claude, Codex, or Claude Code consistent React performance rules during code generation.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Review
prompt_templates.scenario: You want a quick performance check of one component.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React component using the Vercel React best practices skill. Focus on obvious waterfalls, unnecessary re-renders, and bundle issues.

prompt_templates.title: Next.js Data Fetching Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: You are reviewing a page or route handler with async work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Next.js data fetching flow for sequential awaits, missing Promise.all usage, and opportunities for Suspense or composition.

prompt_templates.title: Bundle Optimization Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: You suspect the initial JavaScript bundle is too large.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to identify heavy imports, barrel imports, and non-critical libraries. Recommend scoped changes that reduce initial bundle size.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Refactor Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a careful multi-file performance refactor.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized refactor plan using the Vercel React best practices. Separate critical waterfall fixes from lower-impact rendering and JavaScript improvements.

output_examples.input: Review a Next.js page that awaits user data before rendering the shell.
output_examples.output: The result explains that the layout is blocked by data fetching and recommends moving data loading behind a Suspense boundary.

output_examples.input: Check a component that imports many icons from one package entry point.
output_examples.output: The result flags the barrel import risk and suggests direct imports or Next.js optimized package imports.

output_examples.input: Improve a route handler with authentication, configuration loading, and data fetching.
output_examples.output: The result separates independent operations from dependent ones and recommends starting safe promises earlier.

best_practices: Start with waterfall and bundle-size rules because they usually have the highest performance impact.
Verify each recommendation against the current project structure before changing shared components.
Use the examples as patterns, then adapt names, dependencies, and framework versions to the target app.
anti_patterns: Do not apply every optimization blindly when the code path is small or rarely used.
Do not add memoization or caching without a clear repeated cost or shared request pattern.
Do not move sensitive server data across client boundaries when only a small field is needed.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents write and review React and Next.js code using performance-focused best practices.

faq.question: Can it run performance benchmarks?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and review criteria, but it does not measure runtime performance directly.

faq.question: Does it support both React and Next.js?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes general React rules and Next.js-specific rules for routing, server components, and server actions.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it during refactors?
faq.answer: Yes. The rules are structured for AI agents that generate, review, or refactor application code.

faq.question: Are the code examples production-ready?
faq.answer: They are examples of patterns. Agents should adapt them to the target project and framework version.

faq.question: Does it focus on security?
faq.answer: Only partly. It includes server action authentication guidance, but its main focus is frontend performance.
